content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

September 13, 1995


Thom Stark
Stark Realities
P.O. Box 457
El Cerrito, CA  94530-04576

                                                Re:  Your Request for Advice
                                                     Our File No. A-95-264

Dear Mr. Stark:

     This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
gift and honoraria provisions of the Political Reform Act.1

                                QUESTIONS

     Will the restrictions on the receipt of gifts and honoraria
prevent you from receiving:

     1.  Payments for columns you write for a computer networking
trade journal?

     2.  Payments for lecturing at computer networking trade shows
or for private clients?

     3.  Payments by clients for reimbursement of travel and
lodging while providing training under contract?

                               CONCLUSIONS

     1-3.  If you have maintained bookkeeping records and have
filed tax returns for your computer consulting business, the
payments you receive in each of the situations described above are
not "honoraria," under the Act.  Such payments are "earned income"
and are not limited or prohibited.  These payments are reportable

     1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18995.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814  (916) 322-5660

Advice File No. A-95-264
Page 2


on your candidate's statement of economic interests, as well as
your assuming office statement of economic interests, should you
be successful in your election for El Cerrito City Council.

FACTS

For the past three years you have been a self-employed
computer professional with three separate well-established income
streams. First, as a consultant specializing in computer
networking with a particular concentration on Internet-related
issues. Second, as a contributing editor for Lan Times, a
computer networking trade journal, and third, as a tutorial
lecturer at computer networking trade shows and for private
clients.

As the editor for Lan Times, your name appears on the
masthead and you are responsible for writing a regular column, as
well as various articles, tutorials, interviews and the like, for
which you are paid the same rate as are Lan Times freelance
writers.

In addition, for the past five years you have been a tutorial
lecturer at computer trade shows and for private clients. You are
typically paid for writing materials and presenting two-, four-,
or eight-hour tutorials. At trade shows, you are either paid a
flat fee based on the length of the presentation or are paid a set
fee for each paying attendee at your tutorial. In our August 7,
1995 telephone conversation, you described one such tutorial for
a private client. You indicated that the Ingersoll-Rand
Corporation in New Hampshire was paying you a fee, which included
reimbursement of your travel expenses, to provide a three-hour
presentation to its executives on accessing the internet.

You have expressed concern that California's restrictions on
the receipt of gifts and honoraria might prevent you from
continuing in your computer consultant business if you are elected
to the city council.

ANALYSIS

Section 89501 provides that no elected officeholder or
candidate for local elected office, shall accept any honorarium.
The term "honorarium" means any payment made in consideration for
any speech given, article published, or attendance at any public
or private conference, convention, meeting, social event, meal, or
like gathering. (Section 89502(b).)

"Speech given" as used in Section 89502 means a public
address, oration, or other form of oral presentation, and includes
participation in a panel, seminar, or debate. A "speech given"
does not include a comedic, dramatic, musical, or other similar
artistic performance. (Regulation 18931.1.)

Advice File No. A-95-264
Page 3


"Article published" means a nonfictional written work:

(1) That is produced in connection with any
activity other than the practice of a bona fide
business, trade, or profession; and

(2) That is published in a periodical, journal,
newspaper, newsletter, magazine, pamphlet, or similar
publication.

(Regulation 18931.2(a)
Emphasis Added.)

In addition, any person who receives payment for drafting any
part of an article published, or being identified as an author of,
or contributor to the article, is receiving payment for an
"article published." (Regulation 18931.2(b).)

Your participation as a lecturer at a trade show or on behalf
of a private client before an audience, is a "speech given." In
addition, the articles which you write, or for which you are
identified as a contributing editor for Law Times, meet the
definition of an "article published" as used in Section 89502.

However, the term "honorarium" does not include earned income
for personal services which are customarily provided in connection
with the practice of a bona fide business, trade, or profession,
such as teaching, practicing law, medicine, insurance, real
estate, banking, or building contracting, unless the sole or
predominant activity of the business, trade, or profession is
making speeches. (Section 89502(c).)

A business is considered to be a "bona fide" business if the
owner of the business has maintained books and records of
accounting consistent with the operation of a business and has
filed tax returns in connection with the operation of the claimed
business. (Regulation 18932.1(a).)

You indicated in your letter that you have been writing
articles and serving as the editor for Law Times for approximately
three years. Additionally, you have been actively involved in
making presentations before trade shows or private clients for
about five years. If you have maintained bookkeeping records and

Advice File No. A-95-264
Page 4


have filed tax returns for your business and the predominant
activity of your business is not making speeches,27/ your computer
consulting business is a "bona fide" business.3/

     Moreover, the reimbursement for travel provided under
contract by a client would not be a gift or honoraria provided the
travel is reasonably necessary in connection with a bona fide
business, trade or profession. (Section 89506(d)(3).)

     Accordingly, the payments you receive for writing articles
and serving as the editor for Lan Times, as well as the payments
you receive to provide tutorials at trade shows or private clients
are not "honoraria" under the definition in the Act.

     These payments are, however, income which must be reported on
your candidate's statement of economic interests, as well as your
assuming office statement of economic interests, should you be
successful in your election for city council.

     I hope this has answered your questions sufficiently. If you
need additional information, or wish to discuss this, please feel
free to contact the Commission's Legal Division at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Jeanette E. Turvill
                                            Political Reform Consultant
                                            Legal Division

JET/jt


_________________________

27/  Speechmaking constitutes a predominant business activity when
more than fifty percent of your business activities in the prior
twelve months were dedicated to the preparation and/or delivery of
speeches; or, if more than fifty percent of your business income
is derived from giving speeches. (Regulation 18932.3.) If under
the above two tests, speechmaking is considered the predominant
activity of your business, the payments would be considered
prohibited honoraria.

3/  This letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

CONCLUSION: 1—3.

and are not limited or prohibited.
